title: Introduction to Spatial Planning
text: world’s ecosystems face unprecedented pressures competing human demands. Habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, climate change threaten biodiversity ecosystem services upon human societies depend. Simultaneously, growing global demands food, energy, minerals, infrastructure water intensify competition limited space (Neubert et al. 2025). Marine environments, particular, face growing demands fishing, shipping, aquaculture, renewable energy, mining, tourism—competing ocean space. Spatial planning offers pathway address dual challenges : Protecting biodiversity: Identifying safeguarding areas critical species survival, migration, reproduction, genetic diversity Maintaining ecosystem services: Ensuring continued provision services carbon sequestration, coastal protection, fisheries production, water filtration Supporting sustainable development: Allocating space fishing, aquaculture, renewable energy, economic activities suitable locations Managing multiple uses: Balancing competing demands limited space allocating areas different activities based compatibility, value, sustainability Minimising conflicts: Reducing use-use conflicts spatially separating incompatible activities identifying areas co-location possible Achieving policy commitments: Meeting national international targets Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework’s goal protecting 30% land sea 2030, supporting Sustainable Development Goals economic growth food security Building resilience: Designing spatial plans can withstand adapt climate change evolving socioeconomic conditions Without deliberate spatial planning, development resource use tend follow economic opportunity rather ecological need. result often fragmented habitats, depleted populations, degraded ecosystem services. Spatial planning provides framework making informed, transparent decisions focus conservation efforts allocate space among competing uses. Protected areas remain cornerstone conservation strategy. However, simply designating protected areas sufficient. must ensure protected areas: Represent ecosystems: just spectacular accessible areas, full range habitats species large enough: Providing sufficient area viable populations ecological processes connected: Allowing species movement, gene flow, climate adaptation Persist time: adequate management enforcement Complement management: Working alongside sustainable use restoration efforts Consider multiple uses: Acknowledge range pressures ecosystems require managed access. Achieving goals requires moving beyond ad hoc decisions systematic, evidence-based planning.

title: Introduction to Spatial Planning
text: Spatial prioritisation process identifying actions located efficiently achieve specified objectives. Given limited resources competing demands space, impossible everything, everywhere. Spatial prioritisation tools help decision-makers strategically allocate space investments. fundamental questions spatial prioritisation depend planning context: conservation planning: areas protected achieve biodiversity targets minimum cost? sector allocation multiple-use planning: fishing, aquaculture, energy infrastructure, protected areas located balance ecological, economic, social objectives? zoning: allocate planning units different management zones (e.g., -take reserves, sustainable fishing areas, aquaculture zones) achieve multiple objectives simultaneously? Traditional approaches spatial planning often relied : Ad hoc decisions: Reacting immediate pressures rather strategic allocation Single-sector focus: Optimising one use without considering others Expert opinion alone: Lacking transparent, repeatable methods Opportunistic selection: Protecting allocating areas based availability rather strategic value conservation planning specifically, ad hoc approaches led : Inefficient use resources: Protecting areas contribute little overall conservation goals Gaps representation: Failing include areas important less well-known species habitats Conflict uses: accounting economic social costs Lack transparency: Difficulty explaining justifying decisions stakeholders multiple-use planning, ad hoc approaches resulted : Use-use conflicts: Fishing gear damaged energy infrastructure, shipping disrupting whale migration Suboptimal economic returns: High-value activities excluded suitable areas Environmental degradation: Cumulative, unmanaged impacts overlapping uses Social inequity: Industrial uses displacing small-scale fishers traditional users Systematic conservation planning (Margules Pressey 2000) multiple-use spatial planning (Neubert et al. 2025) address limitations using quantitative methods identify priority areas based explicit objectives constraints.

title: Introduction to Spatial Planning
text: Climate change poses fundamental challenges conservation multiple-use planning. Species distributions shifting, habitats degrading, historical baselines may longer represent future conditions. Simultaneously, climate change affects productivity suitability areas human uses fisheries aquaculture. Climate-smart spatial planning explicitly incorporates climate change considerations prioritisation ensure plans remain effective future conditions (Buenafe et al. 2025). Traditional spatial planning assumes features remain static within planning units. However: biodiversity: Species shifting ranges poleward deeper waters Coral reefs habitats experiencing mass mortality events Ocean temperatures, chemistry, currents changing Protected area networks designed today’s distributions may become ineffective species move economic activities: Fisheries productivity shifting changing ocean conditions Aquaculture suitability changing (temperature, oxygen, disease risk) Extreme weather events threaten infrastructure (ports, offshore installations) Traditional fishing grounds may become less productive shift location Climate-smart planning ensures spatial allocations—whether conservation economic use—remain viable effective future climate scenarios. Climate-smart planning can implemented several approaches shinyplanr, users can select climate layers preferentially weight refugia areas. Climate refugia areas expected experience less climate change impact may serve safe havens biodiversity. can identified using: Projected temperature anomalies Climate velocity (speed conditions shift spatially) Habitat stability metrics Ensemble model projections Given uncertainty climate projections, robust planning aims find solutions perform well across multiple climate scenarios rather optimising single projection.

Depending application configured, targets can set three ways: Individual Targets feature slider, allowing fine-grained control. Adjust slider set percentage feature want protect. Distance Coast: Planning units closer shore generally higher cost, reflecting proxy higher production fisheries value. title: Setting Up shinyplanr for Your Region
text: create_shinyplanr_template() function generates complete, standalone deployment project region. creates project sibling directory current working directory (e.g. ../Fiji/). Open resulting .Rproj file work inside new project. spatial planning, use equal-area projection centred region. ensures planning unit areas correctly calculated. find appropriate CRS: Visit Projection Wizard Enter bounding box coordinates region Select “Equal Area” distortion property Copy PROJ string EPSG code Alternatively, search EPSG.io standard projections used country. CRS Examples Global (default): "ESRI:54009" (Mollweide) Fiji: "+proj=cea +lon_0=178 +lat_ts=-17 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs" Kosrae: "+proj=cea +lon_0=163 +lat_ts=2.8 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs" running create_shinyplanr_template(), self-contained project (using Fiji example): Tip Open Fiji/Fiji.Rproj RStudio work inside deployment project. subsequent scripts run within project, shinyplanr package directory. config/ www/ folders auto-generated run setup/setup-app.R — edit directly. Everything need customise lives inside setup/.

title: Setting Up shinyplanr for Your Region
text: setup/setup-data.R script processes raw spatial data format required shinyplanr. Open setup/setup-data.R RStudio (within deployment project). script contains several sections: set oceandatr = TRUE, template includes code download EEZ boundary: custom boundaries, replace : creates hexagonal grid planning units. Options output include: "sf_hex": Hexagonal sf polygons "sf_square": Square sf polygons "raster": Raster (SpatRaster) format using oceandatr, template downloads several data layers: add spatial data layers: final step combines data saves : Open setup/setup-data.R RStudio (.Rproj open) Review modify needed data Run entire script (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + Enter) Check warnings errors Verify .rda file created Data Download Time first run oceandatr functions may download large datasets. Ensure stable internet connection sufficient disk space.
code: country    <- "Fiji" crs        <- "+proj=cea +lon_0=178 +lat_ts=-17 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs" resolution <- 20000L  # Planning unit size in metres  setup_dir <- "setup"                           # Location of the setup folder data_path <- file.path(setup_dir, "data")      # Raw spatial data files # Get EEZ boundary from Marine Regions database eez <- spatialgridr::get_boundary(name = country, type = "eez") %>%   sf::st_transform(crs = crs) %>%   sf::st_geometry() %>%   sf::st_sf() bndry <- sf::st_read(file.path(data_path, "my_boundary.gpkg")) %>%   sf::st_transform(crs = crs) PUs <- spatialgridr::get_grid(   boundary = eez,   crs = crs,   output = "sf_hex",   resolution = resolution ) # Bathymetry / Depth zones bathymetry <- oceandatr::get_bathymetry(   spatial_grid = PUs,   classify_bathymetry = TRUE ) %>%   sf::st_drop_geometry()  # Geomorphology geomorphology <- oceandatr::get_geomorphology(   spatial_grid = PUs ) %>%   sf::st_drop_geometry()  # Seamounts (with buffer) seamounts <- oceandatr::get_seamounts(   spatial_grid = PUs,   buffer = 30000 ) %>%   sf::st_drop_geometry() # Example: Load habitat data from shapefile habitat <- sf::st_read(file.path(data_path, "habitat.gpkg")) %>%   spatialgridr::get_data_in_grid(     spatial_grid = PUs,     dat = .,     feature_names = "habitat_type",     meth = "average",     cutoff = 0.1   )  # Example: Load raster data my_raster <- terra::rast(file.path(data_path, "my_data.tif")) %>%   spatialgridr::get_data_in_grid(     spatial_grid = PUs,     dat = .,     meth = "average"   ) # Calculate distance to coast cost <- dat_sf %>%   dplyr::select(geometry) %>%   spatialplanr::splnr_get_distCoast(custom_coast = coast) %>%   dplyr::mutate(     Cost_Area = PU_Area,          # Equal area cost     Cost_Distance = coastDistance_km   ) mpas <- spatialplanr::splnr_get_MPAs(   PlanUnits = PUs,    Countries = country ) %>%   sf::st_transform(crs = crs) %>%   spatialgridr::get_data_in_grid(     spatial_grid = PUs,      dat = .,      name = "mpas",      cutoff = 0.5   ) # Combine all features dat_sf <- dplyr::bind_cols(   PUs,   bathymetry,   geomorphology,   seamounts,   cost,   mpas )  # Save to setup/data/ save(dat_sf, bndry, coast,      file = file.path(data_path, paste0(country, "_RawData.rda")))

code: options <- list(      ## General Options   app_title = "Fiji: shinyplanr",   nav_title = "Fiji Spatial Planning",   navbar = list(theme = "dark"),      ## Funder link (opens when funder logo is clicked)   funder_url = "https://spatialplanning.github.io",    ## Logo and data file locations (relative to setup/)   file_logo        = file.path(setup_dir, "logos", "logo.png"),   file_logo2       = file.path(setup_dir, "logos", "logo2.png"),   file_logo3       = file.path(setup_dir, "logos", "logo3.png"),   file_logo_funder = file.path(setup_dir, "logos", "logo_funder.png"),   file_data        = file.path(data_path, paste0(country, "_RawData.rda")),      ## Module switches   mod_1welcome  = TRUE,   mod_2scenario = TRUE,   mod_3compare  = TRUE,   mod_4features = TRUE,   mod_5coverage = TRUE,   mod_6help     = TRUE,      ## UQ logo in welcome footer   show_uq_logo = TRUE,   # Set FALSE to hide the UQ logo    ## Features   include_report = TRUE,   include_bioregion = FALSE,   include_climateChange = FALSE,   include_lockedArea = TRUE,      ## Target grouping   targetsBy = "individual",  # "individual", "category", or "master"      ## Objective function   obj_func = "min_shortfall",  # "min_set" or "min_shortfall"      ## Geographic options   cCRS = "+proj=cea +lon_0=178 +lat_ts=-17 +datum=WGS84" ) include_climateChange = TRUE, climate_change = 1,    # 1 = CPA method percentile = 5,        # For percentile approach direction = -1,        # -1 = low values are refugia refugiaTarget = 1

title: Create a new shinyplanr deployment project — create_shinyplanr_template
code: create_shinyplanr_template(   country,   crs = "ESRI:54009",   oceandatr = TRUE,   resolution = 20000,   include_climate = TRUE,   include_cost = TRUE,   include_mpas = TRUE,   output_dir = file.path("..", country),   use_renv = TRUE,   create_rproj = TRUE )

title: Create a new shinyplanr deployment project — create_shinyplanr_template
text: country Character. Name country/region (e.g., "Fiji", "Kosrae"). Used folder naming default titles. crs Character. Coordinate reference system analysis. Default "ESRI:54009" (Mollweide equal-area projection). Use https://projectionwizard.org find appropriate local CRS. oceandatr Logical. TRUE (default), setup-data.R template include code automatically download data oceandatr (bathymetry, geomorphology, seamounts, knolls, coral habitat, environmental regions). FALSE, creates minimal template manual data entry. resolution Numeric. Planning unit resolution meters. Default 20000 (20 km x 20 km). Smaller values create planning units. include_climate Logical. TRUE (default), includes climate-smart planning options setup-app.R placeholder climate data loading. include_cost Logical. TRUE (default), includes cost layer setup (distance coast, equal area). include_mpas Logical. TRUE (default), includes code fetch marine protected areas WDPA locked-constraints. output_dir Character. Path deployment project folder created. Defaults file.path("..", country), creating sibling directory current working directory. deployer opens folder R project — inside shinyplanr package source. use_renv Logical. TRUE (default), initialises renv new project lock package versions reproducible deployments. Requires renv package installed. Set FALSE skip renv initialisation. create_rproj Logical. TRUE (default), creates RStudio .Rproj file new project easy project opening.
